A SECTION OF ROAD AT THE BRIDGE
The jeep slows suddenly, then stops as Ellis points.
ELLIS
This is where they killed Sergeant
Waters las' month, sir.
Davenport turns. CAMERA PANS to_ the place Ellis
indicates. It is a spot just in front of the jeep, a
wooden stake marks it, otherwise it is no different
from the rest of the gravel road.
DAVENPORT
Why'd you say, they killed him,
Corporal? Who's they?
Ellis eggs the jeep forward. He looks at Davenport,
somewhat surprised.
ELLIS
The Klan, sir --they ain't too
crazy 'bout us Tan yanks down here.

CUT TO:
EXT. HEADQUARTERS 221ST SMOKE GENERATING COMPANY -DAY 27
The jeep with Ellis and Davenport in it skids to a halt,
kicking up dust outside another long wooden building
designated: HQ 221ST SMOKE GENERATING COMPANY. U.S.
CHEMICAL CORPS. A sign is nailed above the building's
only door. Outside, two black GI's are hoeing and dig­
ging in a flower bed that runs the length of the build­
ing on both sides of the door. They're lined with
painted stones. Both men salute as Ellis and Davenport
dismount. Ellis goes. directly to the door and readies
himself to open it for Davenport. Davenport reaches
the door and stops Ellis before he can open it.
Davenport opens it himself and enters. Ellis follows.
CUT TO:
INT. ORDERLY ROOM -DAVENPORT, ELLIS, WASHINGTON -DAY 28
Davenport,. with-Ellis trailing, stops inside the Order­
ly Room, a small office area with several desks and a
single office behind it. Rising from his desk to greet
Davenport with a salute is Sergeant WASHINGTON. He is
a big, dark-skinned Master Sergeant --a proven
veteran if the ribbons he wears from the First War are
any indication. He is clean-shaven, his face jolly,
bis eyes bright, lively. He is all pride and smiles as
he straightens to attention.

. DAVENPORT
Did you see him that day?
ELLIS
Nope --no, sir.
Ellis starts up the jeep.
EXT. BARRACKS -ELLIS/DAVENPORT -DAY
CUT TO:
31*
As Davenport and Ellis approach the barracks of Company
Ca young white LIEUTENANT passes them with a clipboard
in hand. He glances up at Davenport and continues.
Ellis watches. Davenport stops in his tracks and watches
the Lieutenant as he walks away. As if he suddenly
realizes what he passed, the white Officer stops, slowly
turns around and salutes. Davenport coldly returns the
salute and heads for the barracks. Ellis smiles proudly
and follows Davenport .toward the front steps.
DAVENPORT
Did the Sarge drink a lot?
ELLIS
I didn't know him well enough to
offer opinions, sir.
Ellis enters the building ahead of Davenport and holds
the door.

The white Officer is the first person to stare at the jeep
as it passes. In the group of soldiers training, one
soldier stops, looks after the speeding jeep, already
moving into the near distance. He points at it in­
credulously. It sets off a chain reaction. Other soldiers
stop what they are doing, to gaze after the jeep --even
the Sergeant turns around. The white Officer faces the men.

EXT. BASEBALL PARK -DAY

ANOTHER ANGLE -WATERS , MEN
Waters turns to Peterson. The men are s~ddenly quiet,
there is sudden tension.
WATERS
I'm a soldier, Peterson--and
the kind of colored man, that
don't like lazy, shiftless Negroes!
PETERSON
You ain't got to come in here and
call us names!

WATERS, TAYLOR
Waters steps in front of Taylor. His tone low, but
confident.
WATERS
These men don't need time off -­
our boys in North Africa aren't
getting time off --besides we've
got orders to report for a paint
detail at fourteen hundred hours.
TAYLOR
Who issued that order?
WATERS
Major Harris, sir.

neighborhood, Captain? Slumrnin'?

I've sent to Colonel Nivens to

you'personally --my request will
not hurt your army record in any way

consider.
